title: G7 splits over AI access as export controls reshape alliances
subhead: The G7 summit exposed a deepening divide: the US tightens its grip on frontier AI while China pitches free models to the Global South. Meanwhile, Europe's fighter jet program collapses, France refuses to scrap its digital tax, and Chinese researchers make stealth coatings dirt-cheap.

sections.heading: G7 AI summit: two competing visions of who gets frontier models
sections.body:
AI dominated the final day of the G7 summit in Evian-les-Bains, France, but the real story is the split it revealed. Anthropic CEO Dario Amodei and Google DeepMind CEO Demis Hassabis both called for a US-led coalition to set international rules on AI, with Amodei proposing structured access to frontier models and chip export controls that exclude China, according to CNBC. Canadian PM Mark Carney agreed the US could lead such a coalition.

The counter-narrative came from Beijing. China's top diplomat Wang Yi announced an acceleration of a 'global AI cooperation organization' and invited all countries to join, explicitly criticizing 'closed, exclusive and monopolistic approaches to tech development,' as The Next Web reported. The pitch: Chinese open-weight models like DeepSeek and Qwen are free and downloadable by anyone with an internet connection, while US models are increasingly subscription-only and export-controlled.

French President Macron called the US export controls on Anthropic's models a 'strictly nationalist' reaction and warned that switching off access 'like a light switch' could erode the value of American AI firms. He pushed for partnership among democracies instead, backed by a pledge to boost French AI funding as insurance, AP News reported. India, Brazil, Kenya, and South Korea attended as guest nations — the very markets China is courting.
sections.whyItMatters: The global AI governance landscape is bifurcating in real time: a US-led controlled-access model versus a Chinese open-distribution model. For the 6 billion people outside the G7, the question is not which system is better but which one shows up first.

sections.heading: Anthropic export controls: the first AI model access restriction
sections.body:
Last Friday, the US Commerce Department ordered Anthropic to cut foreign national access to its two newest models, Fable 5 and Mythos 5, citing national security concerns. Anthropic complied by shutting off access for everyone — not just foreign nationals — and stated it did not believe the government's action was warranted, as CNBC and AP both reported.

This is the first time US export controls have been applied to AI model access in this way. The controls were reportedly triggered by concerns that the models' cyber capabilities could be weaponized. Anthropic remains locked in negotiations with the Trump administration over the scope of the restrictions.

The fallout was immediate at the G7. Macron forcefully raised the issue, and Cohere CEO Aidan Gomez told AP that 'a number of proposals' on AI governance were discussed, adding: 'the consensus was we need something.' Zach Meyers of Brussels think tank CERNE noted that the Anthropic episode highlighted how Europe and other countries 'can be put in an extremely vulnerable position' if cut off from advanced models.

Meanwhile, MSN/Yahoo reported that SK Telecom, Anthropic's largest investor, may have played a role in the events — though details remain unclear.
sections.whyItMatters: A precedent has been set: frontier AI models can be weaponized by export controls the same way chips and dual-use technology have been. The question every non-US government is now asking is whether access to critical AI capabilities can be revoked overnight.

sections.heading: G7 critical minerals alliance and DeepSeek blacklisting pause
sections.body:
Beyond AI, the G7 agreed on closer cooperation on critical minerals supply chains, including coordinated stockpiling, recycling, and a new cooperation platform supported by the International Energy Agency. Japan PM Shinjiro Takaichi pushed the economic security agenda, tying minerals access to chip and defense supply chain resilience, according to Japan Times and TechRepublic.

The IEA estimates China refines about 70% of the 20 strategic minerals it tracks. China suspended some of its expanded rare earth export controls through November 2026, but licensing rules can change and processing capacity remains heavily concentrated in Chinese hands. The G7 minerals push builds on the Critical Minerals Action Plan from the 2025 Kananaskis summit, with Canada following up with $6.4 billion in projects and offtake agreements last October.

On the enforcement side, Reuters/Yahoo/MSN reported that the US held off adding DeepSeek, CXMT, and more than 100 other firms deemed security risks to the Entity List, specifically to avoid escalating tensions with Beijing. The decision reflects a calculation that blacklisting China's most visible AI startup right now would complicate trade negotiations and the broader diplomatic landscape.
sections.whyItMatters: The G7 is building the institutional architecture for minerals independence, but it is years from scale. The DeepSeek hold-off shows the limits of enforcement: when export controls collide with broader diplomacy, enforcement gets deferred.

sections.heading: Team Gen 6: Europe's fighter jet program restarts without France
sections.body:
Less than a week after the Franco-German Future Combat Air System (FCAS) program collapsed over irreconcilable differences between Airbus and Dassault Aviation, Airbus Defence and Space launched 'Team Gen 6' at the ILA Berlin Airshow on June 11 — a German-Spanish consortium of eight German and five Spanish defense companies aimed at developing a sixth-generation combat jet.

The German firms include Hensoldt, Diehl Defence, MTU Aero Engines, Liebherr, Autoflug, Rohde and Schwarz, and MBDA Germany. Spanish partners include GMV, Grupo Oesia, Indra, ITP Aero, and Sener, as The War Zone and Euronews reported.

Airbus's head of air power Jean-Brice Dumont said the original FCAS had 'drawn a line to 2040' and that 'the world in 2026 is very different from 2017.' The consortium submitted a positioning paper to German Defence Minister Boris Pistorius, urging contracts to be awarded by the second half of 2026. Pistorius called the project 'conceivable' but said Berlin is also evaluating alternatives, including additional F-35 purchases, according to Euronews.

Dassault had demanded the defining role in the now-abandoned NGF, driven by French requirements for carrier operations and nuclear delivery — capabilities neither Germany nor Spain needed. The split leaves France without a sixth-gen fighter path of its own.
sections.whyItMatters: Europe's most ambitious defense cooperation project has fractured along the lines it was supposed to transcend: national industrial interests. Germany and Spain are moving ahead; France is sidelined. The result is a weaker European defense industrial base at a time when Russia is an active threat.

sections.heading: China demystifies stealth coatings as France holds firm on digital tax
sections.body:
Chinese researchers from Foshan University published a peer-reviewed paper in the Journal of Ceramics describing a low-cost radar-absorbing composite made from expanded graphite and titanium dioxide — materials vastly cheaper than the graphene and carbon nanotubes traditionally used in stealth coatings. A separate team led by scientist Jiang Fan from the Naval Equipment Department developed an iron-based coating that reduces infrared signatures by over 80%, using equipment that costs under 50,000 yuan (about $6,970) on Chinese online shopping platforms, as SCMP and Interesting Engineering reported.

The implications are twofold: mass-producible stealth materials lower the barrier for drone swarms and low-cost platforms, and the commercial availability of radar-absorbing coatings — Shenzhen's Star-Navi was reported selling them in 1kg, 5kg, and 10kg containers — means dual-use proliferation controls become nearly impossible to enforce.

In a separate sovereignty standoff, French President Macron stated France would not scrap its digital services tax on US tech giants, according to Reuters/Yahoo. This came just before his G7 meeting with Trump, who had threatened 100% tariffs on French wine if France did not back down. The French digital tax targets revenue generated by US tech companies in France — a direct challenge to American fiscal sovereignty over its own tech firms.
sections.whyItMatters: China is methodically pushing military-relevant dual-use technologies toward commodity pricing, eroding the cost advantage of Western precision manufacturing. France's refusal on the digital tax keeps a fiscal sovereignty dispute with the US simmering inside the G7.

sections.heading: UK faces 200 hostile-state cyberattacks on critical infrastructure
sections.body:
NCSC CEO Richard Horne disclosed that the UK's National Cyber Security Centre managed 200 cyber incidents affecting critical national infrastructure between January and May 2026, with roughly 75% linked to hostile nation-state actors including Russia, China, and Iran. The attacks targeted hospitals, electricity, water, and finance systems, Computer Weekly reported.

Horne warned that by 2028, AI-enabled cyber capabilities will likely be used to exploit known vulnerabilities in legacy technology at scale across critical infrastructure. He framed the situation not as risk management but as 'an ongoing contest between capable adversaries,' adding: 'In cyberspace, we are not preparing for tomorrow's conflicts — to some degree, we are fighting them today.'

The disclosure comes as the UK also moves toward a social media ban for under-16s, effective from spring 2027, which Slate described as going further than Australia's similar law. The ban covers Facebook, Instagram, TikTok, and Snapchat, and also restricts under-18s from accessing 'romantic roleplay' chatbots. An online curfew for 16- and 17-year-olds is also under consideration.
sections.whyItMatters: The 200-incident figure and the 75% state-sponsored attribution put hard numbers on what was previously a qualitative threat assessment. The AI-accelerated timeline — 2028 for scale exploitation — gives infrastructure operators a two-year window that is already closing.

bottomLine: The G7 summit crystallized a fundamental split in global AI governance: the US is building a controlled-access alliance of wealthy democracies, while China is distributing free models to anyone who wants them. The Anthropic export controls set a precedent that alarmed even US allies, and France's Macron made clear he will not accept American AI dominance without European alternatives. Meanwhile, the collapse of the Franco-German fighter jet program and the commercialization of Chinese stealth coatings are reminders that the technology-power landscape is shifting in hardware too — not just software. The UK's disclosure of 200 state-linked cyberattacks on critical infrastructure adds urgency: the contest is not coming, it is underway.
sources: CNBC, AP News, The Next Web, Reuters, Yahoo News, MSN, TechRepublic, Japan Times, The War Zone, Euronews, Computer Weekly, Slate, South China Morning Post, Interesting Engineering, Forbes
